text: The surgical setup includes Maryland forceps with bipolar energy, Cadea forceps, and a thoracic grasper used to position the lung. Two cigar-shaped objects are visible in the chest cavity.
The surgeon begins the right upper lobectomy by dissecting the station seven lymph node along the bronchus intermedius, addressing a small bronchial artery during the procedure.
The surgeon opens the subcarinal space to fully dissect the station seven subcarinal lymph node, a critical structure in thoracic surgery.
The surgeon uses Maryland bipolar forceps to perform a complete dissection, efficiently maneuvering without the need to swap instruments.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to peel the station seven subcarinal lymph node from behind the bronchus intermedius, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the dissection.
The surgeon attempts to remove the station seven lymph node whole, using careful dissection techniques to minimize bleeding and ensure a thorough lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon carefully dissects the station seven lymph node from the pericardium, with the oesophagus visible to the left of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses Maryland bipolar forceps to peel the remaining parts of the station seven lymph node from the esophagus, progressing upwards towards the carina.
The surgeon carefully navigates around small vessels emerging from the bronchus intermedius and nearby structures while continuing the dissection.
The surgeon observes adhesions between the lung tissue and the chest wall, noting that there is no visible evidence of invasion into surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses bipolar Maryland forceps to manipulate tissue, with the forceps occasionally causing slight charring on the tissue surface.
The bronchus intermedius is clearly visible, leading to the right upper lobe bronchus, with the azygos vein seen in the background.
The surgeon identifies a station R-ten hyaluronate lymph node, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon removes lymph nodes around the right upper lobe to facilitate the passage of a stapler behind the lobe, enhancing anatomical visibility and access.
The surgeon carefully removes the lymph nodes whole from station seven, minimizing bleeding and ensuring a thorough lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon positions the Maryland bipolar forceps near the right upper lobe, which is situated above the right upper lobe bronchus. Below this, the bronchus intermedius is visible.
The right main bronchus is visible above the right upper lobe bronchus, passing beneath the ozygous vein to join the left main bronchus and form the trachea.
The surgeon carefully removes the station ten lymph node, ensuring that all blood supply and small lymphatic vessels are addressed to facilitate complete excision of the node.

The surgeon utilizes the Cefolio method for port placement, inserting a 5mm port posteriorly, two 8mm ports in the eighth intercostal space with the camera positioned centrally, and the assistant port placed anteriorly in the ninth or tenth intercostal space.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect and remove the station R-eleven lymph node, ensuring careful handling to maintain the integrity of the node and minimize bleeding.
The surgical field shows the anatomical location between the right upper lobe and the bronchus intermedius, with visible structures positioned relative to these bronchi.
The surgeon prepares to remove a structure completely to reveal the underlying pulmonary artery, ensuring clear visualization of this major vessel.
The pulmonary artery becomes visible to the right of the station R-eleven lymph node during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the lymph node located between the right upper lobe and the bronchus intermedius, providing improved visibility of the pulmonary artery and the posterior descending segmental vessel.
The surgeon identifies Station R-Eleven, an interlobar lymph node located between the upper, lower, and middle lobes of the lung.

The surgeon identifies the R-Eleven S node, which is the superior interlobar lymph node located between the upper, lower, and middle lobes. The inferior version of this node is situated between the middle and lower lobes.
The surgeon carefully removes the entire R-Eleven S lymph node, ensuring complete excision while maintaining visibility of the adjacent pulmonary artery.
The surgeon transitions to the fissure between the upper and middle lobes, preparing to further dissect and expose anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon encounters adhesions in the surgical field, which are visible as fibrous tissue bands connecting the lung lobes.
The surgeon removes adhesions to access the fissure between the upper and middle lobes, potentially allowing visualization of the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon observes the presence of small adhesions but notes that the patient has a well-defined fissure between the lung lobes.
The surgical view clearly displays the anatomical layout: the upper lobe is visible above, the middle lobe is positioned to the right, and the lower lobe is located at the bottom left of the screen.
The surgical view shows the confluence of the fissures, where the pulmonary artery is typically visible.
The surgeon retracts the upper lobe of the lung downward, providing a clearer view of the underlying anatomical structures, including the confluence of the fissures and the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon identifies the azygos vein situated above the area where the station ten lymph node is being excised.
The surgical field reveals the right main bronchus and the superior vena cava (SVC) positioned anteriorly, providing a clear view of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy above the azygos vein, targeting lymph nodes at station R2 and R4.
The posterior limits of the vagus nerve are visible in the surgical field, with surrounding anatomical structures such as the pulmonary artery and confluence of the fissures previously exposed.
The surgeon removes the station R-IV lymph node, visible within the surgical field, with the upper lobe retracted downwards.
The surgeon ensures complete excision of the station ten lymph node from beneath the ozygous vein, carefully removing the tissue in its entirety.
The superior vena cava (SVC) is visible anterior to the surgical field, providing a landmark for orientation during the procedure.
After completing the lymphadenectomy, the surgeon aims to visualize the entire trachea and the posterior aspect of the vagus nerve.
The surgeon retracts the upper lobe of the lung posteriorly to expose and identify the upper lobe vein, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the vein, ensuring not to damage the pulmonary artery, which is positioned just behind the heart.
The surgeon has successfully navigated around the vein and is preparing to use a Tan-Forty-Five stapler with a leader catheter for further surgical action.
The surgeon uses a soft leader catheter to effectively navigate around the pulmonary vessels, ensuring precise manipulation without damaging surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses the leader cath to guide the assistant in maneuvering around the pulmonary vessels, ensuring precise navigation during the procedure.
The surgeon rotates the Tan-Forty-Five stapler around to align it with the vein, ensuring precise positioning for stapling while avoiding the nearby pulmonary artery.
The surgeon fires the Tan-Forty-Five stapler, compressing and cutting the targeted vessel with the assistance of the leader cath.
The surgeon uses the Signia powered stapler to smoothly fire staples, securing the tissue during the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.
The surgeon returns to the right upper lobe bronchus, preparing to manipulate or dissect this anatomical structure.
The right upper lobe and bronchus intermedius are clearly visible in the surgical field, highlighting the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgical field shows a clear view of the pulmonary artery, enhanced by the removal of lymph nodes from station XI and station X.
The surgeon uses a purple 45mm gold tip tri-stapler to secure tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon visually inspects the bronchus intermedius, which is clearly visible in the surgical field, without performing an inflation test.
The surgeon has divided the bronchus and is now excising lymph nodes located just behind it, revealing the truncal branch of the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon identifies and manipulates L-ten hilar lymph nodes, which are located near the pulmonary hilum.
The surgeon approaches the anterior aspect to identify and maneuver around the pulmonary vessels, ensuring clear visualization and access for further dissection.
The surgeon exposes the pulmonary artery, revealing the anterior and apical truncal branches.
The surgeon observes two truncal branches of the pulmonary artery, noting the variation in the arterial anatomy of the right upper lobe.
The surgeon prepares to safely fire the vessel, with the bronchus already divided and no structures visible behind it.
The surgeon uses a leader cast to maneuver through the surgical field, while the assistant operates a Tan-45 stapler to secure the vessels.
The surgical view shows a clear path for the assistant to access the vessels from the low and anterior port, with no reticulation visible in the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon prepares to address the fissure after completing the dissection and management of the bronchus, vein, and artery, with the pulmonary artery's anterior and apical truncal branches previously exposed.
The surgeon uses a purple 60mm stapler to secure the pulmonary tissue, ensuring safe closure of the vessel with no structures behind.
The surgeon performs the final two firings of the stapler, creating straight staple lines along the pulmonary fissure to minimize air leaks.
The surgeon uses a Purple Sixty stapler to create staple lines across the pulmonary fissure, ensuring precise closure to minimize air leaks.
The surgeon prepares for the final firing of the stapler using a purple cartridge, ensuring the staple line is straight to minimize air leaks.
The surgeon completes the fissure using three staple firings, creating straight staple lines to minimize air leaks.
The surgeon prepares to remove the pulmonary lobe using an Endoscopic Wound Protector (EWP), following the completion of staple lines across the fissure.
The surgeon uses a strong cotton bag to remove the resected right upper lobe through the assistant port, completing the lobectomy procedure.
